Observe client's respiratory status
Check residual volume every 4 to 6 hours

questions.questionText: Which is available as an injection to treat obesity
options: Semaglutide (Wegovy)
Orlistat (Ali)
Dronabinol (Marinol)
Metformin
questions.correctAnswer: Semaglutide (Wegovy)
questions.explanationText: Semaglutide (Wegovy) reduces appetite, increases fullness, and slows gastric emptying, which leads to weight loss. Orlistat (Alli), oral med, blocks fat absorption in the intestines, causing some dietary fat to pass through undigested. Dronabinol (Marinol), oral med, stimulates appetite, often in patients with AIDS or cancer-related weight loss. Metformin, oral diabetes med, improves insulin sensitivity and reduces liver glucose production.

questions.questionText: Which is a risk for getting a peptic ulcer
options: history of bulimia
history of NSAID use
drinks green tea
has a glass of wine with dinner each day
questions.correctAnswer: history of NSAID use
questions.explanationText: Peptic ulcer are ulcers of stomach or small intestine.  Bulimia would have ulcers in mouth or esophagus.

questions.questionText: Ulcerative colitis and crohn's are both...
options: Both are inflammatory
questions.correctAnswer: Both are inflammatory
questions.explanationText: Both are inflammatory bowel diseases.  IMPORTANT: Both are not irritable bowel syndrome (catch all phrase for problems with diarrhea, constipation, etc.)

questions.questionText: Difference between ulcerative colitis and Crohn's
options: Ulcerative colitis (UC) affects only the colon, starting at the rectum and spreading continuously, while Crohn’s disease can affect any part of the GI tract and has “skip lesions.” UC inflammation is limited to the inner lining and usually causes bloody diarrhea and urgency, whereas Crohn’s is transmural (all bowel layers) and often causes abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, and malnutrition. UC can be cured by removing the colon, but Crohn’s cannot be cured with surgery and tends to recur.
questions.correctAnswer: Ulcerative colitis (UC) affects only the colon, starting at the rectum and spreading continuously, while Crohn’s disease can affect any part of the GI tract and has “skip lesions.” UC inflammation is limited to the inner lining and usually causes bloody diarrhea and urgency, whereas Crohn’s is transmural (all bowel layers) and often causes abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, and malnutrition. UC can be cured by removing the colon, but Crohn’s cannot be cured with surgery and tends to recur.

questions.questionText: What would you do for Enteroenteric Fistula?
options: Administer antibiotic therapy
questions.correctAnswer: Administer antibiotic therapy
questions.explanationText: An enteroenteric fistula (an opening between two or more portions of the small bowel) is a common complication of Crohn's and can cause infection. Antibiotics will be prescribed to treat and prevent peritonitis. A small bowel obs. can cause serious complications, including ABD distention, fluid and electrolyte imbalance and severe pain. The nurse would insert an NG tube to SX (e.g. Salem Sump) to help remove excessive secretions that tend to accumulate during a bowel obs and to rest the GI tract. Lower GI bleeding due to severe inflammation from Crohn's will often end in blood administration. Inflammation can also cause malabsorption of vital nutrients and with Wednesday's history of Celiac, absorption is already an issue. The presence of undigested food or medicine indicates that nutrients and medications are not being absorbed in the small bowel. Severe malabsorption can cause weight loss requiring nutritional interventions, including enteral supplements or parenteral nutrients.

questions.questionText: The nurse on the unit is also caring for a 17-year old patient with Crohn's.  The nurse finds foul smelling drainage coming from an open area on the patient's abdomen.  The nurse notifies the provider that the patient has developed an enterocutaneous fistula.  Which nursing actions are appropriate for this patient?
options: Monitor the patient for signs of sepsis to prevent septic shock; Apply a skin barrier and pouch to collect drainage and protect the surrounding skin; Administer IV antibiotic therapy as prescribed; Monitor fluid and electrolyte balance to identify dehydration or electrolyte imbalances.
questions.correctAnswer: Monitor the patient for signs of sepsis to prevent septic shock; Apply a skin barrier and pouch to collect drainage and protect the surrounding skin; Administer IV antibiotic therapy as prescribed; Monitor fluid and electrolyte balance to identify dehydration or electrolyte imbalances.
